section.title: The Oracle against Egypt
section.sectionRationale: Within the oracles against the nations, chapters 13 to 23, this is the longest and most surprising: it moves from the LORD striking Egypt's idols and unraveling her economy and wisdom, to Egypt herself worshiping the LORD, and finally to a stunning vision of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed together as one people of God.
section.thesis: The LORD who strikes Egypt's idols, dries her Nile, and confounds her wise men is the same LORD who will one day be known and worshiped by Egypt, so that former enemies, Egypt and Assyria, join Israel as one blessed people under God.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the LORD riding against Egypt's idols and internal collapse; the Nile drying up and every trade failing; the wise counselors of Egypt exposed as fools; Egypt trembling before Judah and then turning to worship the LORD; and the highway of blessing joining Egypt, Assyria, and Israel.

units.label: The LORD rides against Egypt's idols

verses.text: This is the burden against Egypt: Behold, the LORD rides on a swift cloud; He is coming to Egypt. The idols of Egypt will tremble before Him, and the hearts of the Egyptians will melt within them.
“So I will incite Egyptian against Egyptian; brother will fight against brother, neighbor against neighbor, city against city, and kingdom against kingdom.
Then the {{ruach|spirit}} of the Egyptians will be emptied out from among them, and I will frustrate their plans, so that they will resort to idols and spirits of the dead, to mediums and spiritists.
I will deliver the Egyptians into the hands of harsh masters, and a fierce king will rule over them,” declares the Lord GOD of Hosts.

units.label: The Nile dries up and every trade fails

verses.text: The waters of the Nile will dry up, and the riverbed will be parched and empty.
The canals will stink; the streams of Egypt will trickle and dry up; the reeds and rushes will wither.
The bulrushes by the Nile, by the mouth of the river, and all the fields sown along the Nile, will wither, blow away, and be no more.
Then the fishermen will mourn, all who cast a hook into the Nile will lament, and those who spread nets on the waters will pine away.
The workers in flax will be dismayed, and the weavers of fine linen will turn pale.
The workers in cloth will be dejected, and all the hired workers will be sick at heart.

units.label: Egypt's wise counselors exposed as fools

verses.text: The princes of Zoan are mere fools; Pharaoh's wise counselors give senseless advice. How can you say to Pharaoh, “I am one of the wise, a son of eastern kings”?
Where are your wise men now? Let them tell you and reveal what the LORD of Hosts has planned against Egypt.
The princes of Zoan have become fools; the princes of Memphis are deceived. The cornerstones of her tribes have led Egypt astray.
The LORD has poured into her a spirit of confusion. Egypt has been led astray in all she does, as a drunkard staggers through his own vomit.
There is nothing Egypt can do, head or tail, palm or reed.

units.label: Egypt trembles, then turns to the LORD

verses.text: In that day the Egyptians will be like women. They will tremble with fear beneath the uplifted hand of the LORD of Hosts, when He brandishes it against them.
The land of Judah will bring terror to Egypt; whenever Judah is mentioned, Egypt will tremble over what the LORD of Hosts has planned against it.
In that day five cities in the land of Egypt will speak the language of Canaan and swear allegiance to the LORD of Hosts. One of them will be called the City of the Sun.
In that day there will be an altar to the LORD in the center of the land of Egypt, and a pillar to the LORD near her border.
It will be a sign and a witness to the LORD of Hosts in the land of Egypt. When they cry out to the LORD because of their oppressors, He will send them a savior and defender to rescue them.
The LORD will make Himself known to Egypt, and on that day Egypt will acknowledge the LORD. They will worship with sacrifices and offerings; they will make vows to the LORD and fulfill them.
And the LORD will strike Egypt with a plague; He will strike them but heal them. They will turn to the LORD, and He will hear their prayers and heal them.

units.label: A highway of blessing for Egypt, Assyria, and Israel

verses.text: In that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria. The Assyrians will go to Egypt, and the Egyptians to Assyria. The Egyptians and Assyrians will worship together.
In that day Israel will join a three-party alliance with Egypt and Assyria, a blessing upon the earth.
The LORD of Hosts will bless them, saying, “{{barak|Blessed}} be Egypt My people, Assyria My handiwork, and Israel My inheritance.”

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, or wind. Egypt's spirit is emptied out and later a spirit of confusion is poured into her, showing that even a nation's inner resolve and wisdom lie fully within the LORD's control, to remove or to confuse as He wills.

barak.gloss: To bless, to speak good and grant favor. The astonishing final word of this oracle is that the LORD blesses Egypt and Assyria by name alongside Israel, using covenant language once reserved for Abraham's family and now extended to former enemies.

basic.context: This is the longest and most surprising of Isaiah's oracles against the nations. It begins as we would expect, with the LORD striking Egypt's idols, setting Egyptians against one another, drying up the Nile that Egypt's whole economy depended on, and exposing Pharaoh's famous wise men as fools. But it does not end there; astonishingly, it turns to Egypt worshiping the LORD, being healed by Him, and finally joining Assyria and Israel as three nations equally blessed by God.
basic.hermeneutics: The judgment portion, verses 1 to 15, reflects real historical realities: Egypt's chronic internal strife, its dependence on the Nile, and the failure of its renowned wisdom tradition when Assyria and later powers pressed against it. The concluding vision, verses 18 to 25, reaches beyond any single historical fulfillment toward the ingathering of the nations that the New Testament associates with the gospel; readers rightly debate how much of this is fulfilled already and how much awaits final consummation.
basic.originalLanguages: Egypt's ruach, spirit, is first emptied out in panic, then filled with a spirit of confusion, showing her collapse to be entirely under the LORD's hand. The oracle's stunning climax uses barak, bless, the same covenant word given to Abraham, now spoken over Egypt and Assyria by name alongside Israel, God's inheritance.
basic.typology: The highway joining once-hostile Egypt and Assyria anticipates the way Christ breaks down every wall of hostility between peoples, Ephesians 2:14, uniting former enemies into one people of God. Egypt's altar and pillar to the LORD in the heart of a pagan land foreshadow worship no longer confined to one place but offered by all nations through Christ.
basic.christ: This chapter's vision of Egypt healed after being struck, and of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed as one, anticipates the reconciling work of Christ, who breaks down the dividing wall of hostility between peoples and creates in Himself one new humanity, Ephesians 2:14-16. The savior and defender the LORD sends to rescue oppressed Egypt, verse 20, previews the greater Savior sent for all nations.
basic.god: God rules Egypt's internal politics, her river, and her wisdom as thoroughly as He rules Israel's history; nothing in Egypt's famed civilization escapes His hand. Yet the same hand that strikes also heals, and the same LORD who judges also draws Egypt into covenant blessing, showing that His purposes for the nations run through judgment toward redemption, not judgment alone.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 7 to 12 recalls the LORD's earlier plagues on Egypt's gods, now echoed and then reversed here. Ephesians 2:14-16 describes Christ breaking down hostility between peoples, fulfilling this chapter's highway of union. Psalm 87:4 anticipates Egypt and Babylon being counted among those who know the LORD. Revelation 7:9 pictures the full ingathering of every nation in worship.
basic.oneStory: This oracle moves from judgment on Egypt's idols and wisdom to an astonishing vision of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed together as God's people. It anticipates the whole sweep of the gospel story, in which Christ reconciles once-hostile nations into one new humanity, so that even ancient enemies become fellow heirs of God's blessing.

context.literary: By far the longest oracle in the collection of chapters 13 to 23, it follows a clear arc from judgment, verses 1 to 15, through Egypt's fear before Judah, verses 16 to 17, into worship and healing, verses 18 to 22, and finally into a vision of three-nation covenant blessing, verses 23 to 25, a structure of descent and then ascent unique among these oracles.
context.historical: Egypt in this period suffered genuine chronic instability, rival dynasties, and internal strife, and depended entirely on the Nile's flooding for agriculture, so a failed Nile meant economic collapse; Egypt's wise men were famous throughout the ancient world, making their exposed folly here especially pointed. The oracle likely addresses the eighth to seventh century BC world of Assyrian expansion, when Judah's neighbors debated whether to lean on Egypt for help.
context.canonical: Uniquely among the oracles against the nations, this chapter ends not merely in judgment but in blessing extended to the very nations judged, anticipating the missionary hope found in Psalm 87, the prophetic vision of nations streaming to Zion in Isaiah 2 and 60, and ultimately the New Testament's global, multiethnic vision of the people of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD who judges Egypt's idols and wisdom is also able to redeem her, warning Judah against trusting Egyptian power while promising that even Egypt will one day belong to the LORD alongside Israel and Assyria.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The judgment section describes real historical dynamics in ancient Egypt; the closing vision of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed together describes a future hope rather than prescribing a specific political program. The abiding truth is that no nation's opposition to God is final and that His redemptive purposes extend to all peoples.

debates.question: How literally and how soon is the vision of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed together, verses 23 to 25, meant to be fulfilled?

views.label: A near historical hope of regional peace
views.case: Some read this as envisioning a real, if partial, era of peace and shared worship among these ancient powers, glimpsed at various points when all three lived under a common regional order.

views.label: An eschatological picture fulfilled in the church and consummated at the end
views.case: Others see this as a prophetic picture whose true fulfillment is the multiethnic people of God created in Christ, Ephesians 2, and finally realized when every nation worships together in the new creation, Revelation 7:9.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that God's purposes for the nations move toward reconciliation and shared worship; the New Testament's application of similar unity language to the church, Ephesians 2, suggests the ultimate horizon is the gospel bringing former enemies into one family in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Egypt's ruach, spirit, is first drained away in panic and then filled with a spirit of confusion, so that her failure of nerve and of counsel is traced directly to the LORD's action, not mere circumstance. The chapter's astonishing final word uses barak, bless, the covenant word first spoken over Abraham, now spoken over Egypt and Assyria by name, showing the LORD's redemptive intentions reaching to the very nations that opposed Him.

typology.type: The highway from Egypt to Assyria, and shared worship
typology.fulfillment: Christ's breaking down of the dividing wall of hostility between peoples, creating one new humanity from former enemies, Ephesians 2:14-16.

typology.type: The altar and pillar to the LORD within Egypt itself
typology.fulfillment: Worship no longer confined to Jerusalem but offered by every nation through Christ, John 4:21-24, Malachi 1:11.

typology.type: The savior and defender sent to rescue oppressed Egypt
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Savior sent not to Israel only but to deliver people from every nation, John 3:16-17.

study.christ: This chapter's turn from judgment to healing, verse 22, He will strike them but heal them, previews the pattern of the gospel, where God's righteous judgment on sin gives way to gracious restoration in Christ. The vision of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed together, once bitter enemies now called My people, My handiwork, and My inheritance, anticipates Paul's declaration that Christ has broken down the dividing wall of hostility, creating in Himself one new humanity out of formerly hostile peoples, Ephesians 2:14-16, the final answer to this oracle's astonishing hope.
study.god: God's sovereignty extends fully into Egypt's internal politics, her Nile, and her famous wisdom tradition, none of which can shield her from His hand. Yet His purposes are never merely destructive; the same LORD who strikes Egypt with plague also heals her when she turns to Him, revealing a God whose judgments serve His larger redemptive plan to bless the nations, not merely to punish them.

crossReferences.note: The earlier plagues that struck Egypt's gods and Pharaoh, echoed and answered by this later oracle of Egypt's judgment and eventual healing.
Egypt, called Rahab here, is numbered among those who know the LORD, anticipating this chapter's vision of Egypt as God's people.
Christ breaks down the dividing wall of hostility between peoples, fulfilling the highway of shared worship between Egypt and Assyria.
A multitude from every nation before the throne, the final, complete fulfillment of this oracle's vision of Egypt, Assyria, and Israel blessed as one.

study.oneStory: This oracle traces Egypt from judgment on her idols and wisdom to healing, worship, and covenant blessing alongside Assyria and Israel. It is a striking preview of the whole biblical story's direction: God's judgment on human pride and idolatry is never His last word, and His purpose is to gather even the nations that opposed Him into one blessed family, a purpose finally accomplished when Christ reconciles former enemies into a single new humanity.

quiz.q: What happens to Egypt's internal stability at the start of this oracle?

options.text: The LORD incites Egyptian against Egyptian, causing internal strife and civil conflict.
options.note: Verse 2 describes exactly this, brother against brother, city against city.

options.text: Egypt immediately unites under a single strong ruler.
options.note: The opposite; the oracle describes internal fragmentation, not unity.

options.text: Egypt forms a peace treaty with all her neighbors.
options.note: Not found in the judgment section of this oracle.

options.text: Egypt's army is destroyed in a single battle.
options.note: Military defeat is implied later, but the specific opening image is internal civil strife.

quiz.q: What happens to Egypt's famous wise men and counselors?

options.text: They are exposed as fools who give senseless advice and cannot discern the LORD's plan.
options.note: Verses 11 to 14 directly expose the folly of Pharaoh's wise counselors.

options.text: They successfully predict everything the LORD is doing.
options.note: The text says the opposite; their wisdom fails completely.

options.text: They are praised as the wisest in the ancient world.
options.note: This was their reputation before the oracle, but the passage instead exposes their failure.

options.text: They are replaced by wise men from Assyria.
options.note: Not stated; the focus is on their own folly being exposed, not replacement.

quiz.q: What surprising turn does the oracle take starting in verse 18?

options.text: Egyptian cities begin to swear allegiance to the LORD and worship Him with sacrifices.
options.note: Verses 18 to 21 describe exactly this turn toward worship of the LORD in Egypt.

options.text: Egypt declares permanent war against Judah.
options.note: The opposite; Egypt turns toward worship of the LORD, not war.

options.text: Egypt is completely destroyed with no future mentioned.
options.note: The oracle instead describes Egypt's healing and restoration to the LORD.

options.text: Egypt begins worshiping a new set of foreign idols.
options.note: The turn is specifically toward the LORD, away from idols, not toward new ones.

quiz.q: How does the oracle conclude in verses 23 to 25?

options.text: Egypt, Assyria, and Israel are blessed together as God's people, His handiwork, and His inheritance.
options.note: Verse 25 gives this astonishing three-nation blessing by name.

options.text: Israel is destroyed while Egypt and Assyria are spared.
options.note: The opposite; all three are blessed together, not one destroyed.

options.text: Assyria conquers both Egypt and Israel permanently.
options.note: Conquest is not the final picture; blessing and shared worship are.

options.text: A treaty is signed but no divine blessing is mentioned.
options.note: There is unity described, but the text specifically frames it as the LORD's own blessing, not merely a political treaty.

quiz.q: How does this oracle's ending anticipate the New Testament?

options.text: It previews Christ breaking down hostility between peoples to create one new humanity.
options.note: Ephesians 2:14-16 describes exactly this reconciling work, fulfilling the chapter's vision.

options.text: It shows that Gentile nations can never truly belong to God's people.
options.note: The opposite is true; Egypt and Assyria are explicitly called God's people and handiwork.

options.text: It predicts a purely political alliance with no spiritual significance.
options.note: The language used, My people, My inheritance, is deliberately covenantal and spiritual, not merely political.

options.text: It is only about ancient trade routes between Egypt and Assyria.
options.note: A highway is mentioned, but the passage's climax is shared worship and covenant blessing, not commerce.

questions: Egypt's famous wisdom was exposed as folly before the LORD. Where might you be trusting in your own wisdom or reputation rather than seeking God's counsel?
The LORD struck Egypt but also healed her when she turned to Him. Where have you experienced God's discipline followed by His restoration in your own life?
Former enemies, Egypt and Assyria, are pictured worshiping together and being called God's people. Is there a person or group you consider too far gone for God's grace to reach?
Egypt's whole economy collapsed when the Nile, the thing she depended on most, failed. What is the 'Nile' in your life, the resource you are quietly depending on more than God?
This chapter ends with astonishing, undeserved blessing pronounced over once-hostile nations. How does this shape the way you pray for people or nations that seem far from God right now?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints an em dash before head or tail, palm or reed, it has been rendered as a comma per house style; the phrase is an idiom for total helplessness, covering every part of society from top to bottom.

The City of the Sun likely refers to Heliopolis, an ancient center of Egyptian sun worship; its inclusion in a city that will swear allegiance to the LORD sharpens the contrast between former idolatry and future worship of the true God.
Where BSB prints an em dash before a blessing upon the earth, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 19:1-25. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
